g.data-narration: Welcome to Theory of Computation! Today we explore how computers recognize patterns through Finite Automata and Regular Expressions.
g.text: Foundation of Pattern Recognition in Computation

g.data-narration: A Finite Automaton is a mathematical model with states, transitions, start state, and accepting states that processes input strings character by character.
g.text: A mathematical model of computation with a finite number of states.
Processes input strings one symbol at a time, moving between states.
If it ends in an accepting state, the input string is ACCEPTED.

g.data-narration: Regular Expressions provide algebraic notation using union, concatenation, and Kleene star to describe patterns of strings compactly.
g.text: Algebraic notation to describe sets of strings using patterns.
Example: (a|b)*abb describes all strings ending with "abb"

g.data-narration: A Deterministic Finite Automaton is a 5-tuple where the transition function maps each state-symbol pair to exactly one next state.
g.text: DFA = (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F) where each component has specific meaning:
q₀ - START state, F - ACCEPTING states

g.data-narration: A Non-Deterministic Finite Automaton allows zero, one, or multiple transitions for each state-symbol pair, using the power set in its transition function.
g.text: NFA = (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F) where δ maps to power set of Q:
δ: Q × Σ → P(Q) - TRANSITION FUNCTION maps to SETS of states

g.data-narration: Despite their differences, DFA and NFA accept exactly the same class of languages - regular languages. This equivalence is proven by Kleene's theorem.
g.text: DFA vs NFA - Key Differences & Equivalence
Kleene's Theorem: DFA ≡ NFA (accept same regular languages)

g.data-narration: The Subset Construction algorithm converts an NFA to DFA by creating states that represent subsets of NFA states. Always compute ε-closures first!
g.text: NFA to DFA: Subset Construction Algorithm
Converts NFA states to DFA states representing subsets of NFA states:
Step 1: Compute ε-closure of initial state
Step 2: For each DFA state and input, compute reachable NFA states
Step 3: Continue until all subsets explored

g.data-narration: Regular Expressions use three basic operations: Union (|), Concatenation (.), and Kleene Star (*) to build complex patterns from simple symbols.
g.text: Basic: ε (empty string), ∅ (empty set), a (single symbol)

g.data-narration: Thompson's Construction systematically converts any regular expression to an NFA using patterns for basic operations and combining them incrementally.
State Elimination converts a DFA to Regular Expression by adding new start/final states and eliminating intermediate states, labeling edges with regex.
g.text: DFA to RE: State Elimination Method
Systematic algorithm to derive regular expression from DFA:
1. Add new initial state q_start and final state q_accept
2. Eliminate intermediate states one by one
→ Final: RE from start to accept

g.data-narration: Regular expressions follow important algebraic laws: commutativity, associativity, distributivity, with ε as identity and ∅ as annihilator.
The Pumping Lemma states that regular languages can be 'pumped' - any long enough string can be split into parts where middle section can repeat.
g.text: If L is regular, ∃ pumping length P such that ∀w∈L with |w|≥P:
w = xyz, where y≠ε, |xy|≤P, and xyⁱz ∈ L for all i≥0
Used to PROVE non-regularity: assume regular → find counterexample → not regular!

g.data-narration: Finite automata are used in lexical analyzers for compilers, text editors for pattern matching, and network protocols for verification.
In summary: FA and RE are equivalent representations of regular languages. Use DFA/NFA for implementation and RE for compact pattern description.
g.text: FA (5-tuple) ≡ RE (Kleene) | DFA: deterministic | NFA: non-deterministic | Subset Construction: NFA→DFA | Thompson's: RE→NFA | State Elimination: DFA→RE

g.data-narration: Remember: Always verify DFA completeness, compute ε-closures in NFA-to-DFA, and use pumping lemma carefully to prove non-regularity.
g.text: • Always ensure DFA has transitions for ALL symbols (add dead state if needed)
• Compute ε-closures FIRST when converting NFA to DFA
• Don't confuse R* (zero or more) with R+ (one or more)
